strands.title: Security Fixes (Immediate)
strands.content: Two one-line fixes, independent of all refactor work. Bug 1: decorators.py @login_required missing is_active check — add abort(403) after is_authenticated check. Bug 2: api/routes.py API key validation missing is_active check — add key.user.is_active guard with abort(401). Both are ready to merge now, no migration needed, no refactor dependency.

key_claims.text: Bug 1 fix: one line in decorators.py — abort(403) if not current_user.is_active
Bug 2 fix: one line in api/routes.py — abort(401) if not key.user.is_active
Both fixes are independent of the refactor — merge now

strands.title: OAuth2 Bolt-On (Option A)
strands.content:
Recommended path: fix bugs + bolt-on OAuth2 without the big refactor. Scope: auth/oauth.py (~140 lines, zero new deps), upsert_oauth_user in auth/oauth.py (not app.py), two callback routes in app.py (/oauth/<provider>/start, /oauth/<provider>/callback), OAuthIdentity model in models.py, one migration. Estimated ~200 lines, shippable in a day or two.

upsert_oauth_user lives in auth/oauth.py and handles both lookup paths: (1) OAuthIdentity(provider, provider_user_id) with joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user) — fixes the lazy='raise' bug inline; (2) User.email match if user_info.email is not None. Email-merge is safe for Google/GitHub (both verify). Google email_verified gate enforced in fetch_user_info before email is returned. GitHub /user/emails returns only verified+primary.

Key behavioral invariants preserved from prior design: provider_user_id (not email) is stable key; state token validated with hmac.compare_digest, popped immediately; no refresh tokens stored; access token discarded after user info fetch; _raise_for_provider_error omits resp.text; Google email_verified=False sets email=None; GitHub /user/emails soft-fails to None.

key_claims.text: Option A scope: ~200 lines across auth/oauth.py, app.py routes, models.py, 1 migration
upsert_oauth_user belongs in auth/oauth.py, not app.py — keeps OAuth logic self-contained
joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user) on the lookup query — lazy='raise' bug fixed inline
Zero new dependencies — requests (existing) + stdlib sufficient
Email-merge safe for Google/GitHub; disabled if provider does not verify emails

strands.title: Refactor Decision: Deferred
strands.content:
The full auth/ consolidation (service.py, backends.py, middleware.py, g.identity, AnonymousIdentity, AuthRequest/AuthResult, 5-week migration) is explicitly deferred. It is not cancelled — the sprawl across 5 files is real and will cause problems as auth grows — but it has no urgency. Security bugs have urgency. OAuth2 has product urgency. Refactor is an engineering quality goal for a future sprint.

The over-engineering risk was: AuthRequest/AuthResult frozen dataclasses, AnonymousIdentity, g.identity as unified identity object. These are good ideas for Option B but should not be implemented for Option A. Flask-Login's current_user is sufficient until it isn't.

Option B remains valid if: auth sprawl causes bugs, or onboarding friction increases, or a third auth method is added. Trigger: not a calendar date, but a pain signal.

key_claims.text: Full auth/ refactor is deferred — not cancelled, but no urgency this sprint
AuthRequest/AuthResult, AnonymousIdentity, g.identity: do NOT implement for Option A
Flask-Login current_user is sufficient identity object until Option B is triggered

strands.title: Testing Strategy (Revised)
strands.content:
With refactor deferred, test surface shrinks significantly. No tests for old scattered auth logic (still dead code). No tests needed for AuthRequest/AuthResult/AnonymousIdentity (not being built).

Actual test targets for Option A: (1) test_decorators.py: Bug 1 regression — suspended user gets 403 not redirect, anonymous gets 401. (2) test_oauth.py: unit tests with mocked requests. Covers: GitHub exchange_code happy path (provider_user_id is str), GitHub /user/emails soft-fail, Google email_verified=False → email None, OAuthError on provider error, state mismatch returns failure, upsert_oauth_user lookup priority. (3) One integration test: mocks only HTTP calls, exercises route→upsert_oauth_user→DB→session path.

Existing integration tests remain regression signal for any future migration work.

strands.title: Current Auth Fragmentation
strands.content: Auth logic was scattered across 5 locations: login/logout in app.py, User model + password hashing in models.py, @login_required in decorators.py, API key validation in api/routes.py, service account token logic in jobs/worker.py. Three distinct auth methods (session, API key, service token) handled ad-hoc. Two confirmed security bugs: (1) decorators.py @login_required missing is_active check — session outlives account disable, FIXED in skeleton; (2) api/routes.py API key validation ignores is_active/revoked_at — FIXED previously, joinedload in service.py addresses performance follow-up. OAuth2 is additive — terminates in a session, not a fourth request-time auth method. Old scattered logic (app.py, api/routes.py, jobs/worker.py) is dead code — no tests should be written for it.

key_claims.text: Auth is split across at least 5 files
Three request-time auth methods: session, API key, service token — OAuth2 is not a fourth
Bug 1 (decorators.py is_active) FIXED in skeleton — abort(403) added
Bug 2 (api/routes.py) fixed; joinedload in _resolve_api_key addresses 2-query problem
Old scattered auth logic is dead code — do not write tests for it

strands.title: Target Architecture: auth/ Package
strands.content: Confirmed file structure: __init__.py, models.py, backends.py, service.py, decorators.py, middleware.py, exceptions.py, routes.py (OAuth callbacks), oauth.py (provider HTTP logic). Key design: g.identity as single identity object; AnonymousIdentity as falsy default; middleware never raises; Flask-Login demoted to session backend only; auth/service.py is the single public entry point. auth/oauth.py holds provider HTTP logic — deliberate deviation from prior backends.py plan. OAuth2 does not touch middleware, decorators, g.identity, or _resolve_session. ApiKey relationships use lazy='raise' — enforces explicit joinedload discipline. OAuthIdentity.user also lazy='raise' — known bug in _upsert_oauth_user needs explicit joinedload or User.query.get().

key_claims.text: auth/service.py is the single public entry point
auth/oauth.py holds provider HTTP logic — deliberate deviation from prior plan
OAuth2 does not touch middleware, decorators, g.identity, or _resolve_session
ApiKey.user and OAuthIdentity.user both lazy='raise' — joinedload required at all access sites

strands.title: auth/oauth.py — Complete Implementation
strands.content:
auth/oauth.py is now fully written (~140 lines). Zero new dependencies: requests (existing dep) for HTTP, stdlib hmac/secrets/urllib.parse for crypto and URL construction. ops constraint (no new deps until next release cycle) is satisfied by design — this was already the plan.

Structure: _PROVIDERS dict (table-driven config for google/github). OAuthUserInfo frozen dataclass: provider, provider_user_id (str, stable key), email, name, avatar_url. Public functions: generate_state_token() → secrets.token_urlsafe(32); validate_state_token(expected, received) → hmac.compare_digest; build_authorization_url(provider, client_id, redirect_uri, state) → URL string; exchange_code(...) → access_token str; fetch_user_info(provider, access_token) → OAuthUserInfo.

Google specifics: OIDC userinfo endpoint; email_verified field checked — if False, email set to None (email-merge only on verified); sub field is provider_user_id. access_type=online (no refresh tokens stored).

GitHub specifics: numeric id cast to str for provider_user_id; email fetched from /user; if None, falls back to _fetch_github_primary_email() hitting /user/emails, returns only verified+primary entry. Soft failure (returns None) if /user/emails call fails.

_raise_for_provider_error: checks resp.ok, raises OAuthError with HTTP status — deliberately omits resp.text (PII/token risk). GitHub requires Accept: application/json on token exchange to avoid form-encoded response.

Refresh tokens: deliberately not stored (access_type=online for Google, no refresh scope for GitHub). Access token used once to fetch user info, then discarded. This is correct for login-only OAuth2. Refresh tokens would only be needed for acting on behalf of user (post to GitHub, access Drive) — a future feature that would then justify a library.

Line count estimate: oauth.py ~140, service.py additions ~40, routes.py ~50, migration ~25, test_oauth.py ~150, integration test ~60. Total ~465 lines for complete OAuth2 feature.

key_claims.text: auth/oauth.py is complete — zero new dependencies required
ops constraint (no new deps) is satisfied — requests and stdlib are sufficient
Google: email set to None if email_verified=False — email-merge guard
GitHub: provider_user_id is str(data['id']) — cast from int, consistent with OAuthUserInfo contract
GitHub: _fetch_github_primary_email soft-fails (returns None) if /user/emails call fails
Refresh tokens not stored — access token discarded after user info fetch; correct for login-only OAuth2
Total OAuth2 feature ~465 lines across 6 files
_raise_for_provider_error omits resp.text — deliberate PII/token protection

strands.title: auth/service.py & _upsert_oauth_user
strands.content:
service.py: module-level façade. AuthRequest (frozen dataclass: method, credential, identifier), AuthResult (frozen dataclass: success, identity, failure_reason — internal only). Public interface: authenticate(), resolve_identity(), invalidate_session(), rotate_api_key(), revoke_service_token(), initiate_oauth(), complete_oauth(). complete_oauth() returns AuthResult — same shape as authenticate(). Password flow runs check_password on dummy hash when user not found (timing guard). API key flow uses joinedload(ApiKey.user) — single round trip. failure_reason is internal — never serialized to API responses. rotate_api_key returns plaintext exactly once.

initiate_oauth(): generates state token via oauth.generate_state_token(), stores in Flask session, builds redirect URL via oauth.build_authorization_url(). complete_oauth(): validates state with hmac.compare_digest (pops from session immediately after), calls oauth.exchange_code(), oauth.fetch_user_info(), _upsert_oauth_user(), logs in via Flask-Login, returns AuthResult.

Known bug: _upsert_oauth_user accesses oauth_identity.user after OAuthIdentity filter_by query — will raise with lazy='raise'. Fix options: (A) joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user) on the query, (B) replace attribute access with User.query.get(oauth_identity.user_id). Not yet fixed — will surface as test failure in TestCompleteOAuthInService against real DB fixture, by design.

key_claims.text: complete_oauth() returns AuthResult — same shape as authenticate()
Password flow runs check_password on dummy hash when user not found — enumeration guard
Known bug: _upsert_oauth_user oauth_identity.user access will raise with lazy='raise'
State token popped from session immediately after hmac.compare_digest check
failure_reason never serialized to API responses

strands.title: OAuth2 & Security Policy
strands.content:
OAuth2 is account provisioning terminating in a session — not a request-time auth method. OAuthIdentity table: id, user_id FK, provider, provider_user_id, created_at, last_used_at. UniqueConstraint on (provider, provider_user_id). lazy='raise' on user relationship.

_upsert_oauth_user lookup priority: (1) OAuthIdentity(provider, provider_user_id) — stable, preferred. (2) User.email match — SECURITY POLICY: auto-link on matching email. Google and GitHub both verify emails — safe. Google additionally gates on email_verified field in oauth.py before returning email. If a provider does NOT verify emails, disable email-merge. Policy must be explicit in code comment.

State token: generated in initiate_oauth, stored in Flask session, validated with hmac.compare_digest, popped immediately after check. Callback errors: redirect to login with generic flash — never surface failure_reason. 401/403 asymmetry: 401 for API key failures even when account disabled; 403 for session-based suspended account (identity already known). One schema migration needed: oauth_identities table.

No refresh tokens stored (login-only OAuth2). Access token discarded after user info fetch. Future need for acting-on-behalf-of-user would justify an OAuth library — not in scope.

key_claims.text: OAuth2 is account provisioning, not a request-time auth method — terminates in a session
Email-merge safe for Google/GitHub (both verify) — Google's email_verified check enforced in oauth.py
State token validated with hmac.compare_digest — not == operator
401 for API key failures, 403 for session-based suspended account — asymmetry is deliberate
One schema migration needed: oauth_identities table
No refresh tokens — access token used once and discarded; correct for login-only scope

strands.title: Testing Strategy
strands.content:
Four-layer strategy. Test the new auth/ package directly; treat old scattered code as dead (no tests for app.py login, api/routes.py inline check, jobs/worker.py token logic). Existing integration tests (hardcoded test user login) are the regression safety net for weeks 3–5 cutover — don't touch them.

Layer 1: tests/auth/test_service.py — unit tests, pytest + unittest.mock, no live DB, mock at SQLAlchemy query layer. Covers: password auth (valid, wrong password, inactive user → 403 not 401, nonexistent user runs dummy hash), API key auth (valid with joinedload verified, revoked key → 401, disabled account → 401 not 403), resolve_identity (returns AnonymousIdentity not None, never raises on garbage token).

Layer 2: tests/auth/test_oauth.py — unit tests with mocked requests.post/get. Covers: GitHub exchange_code happy path (provider_user_id is str not int), GitHub fetches /user/emails when email is None (picks verified+primary), OAuthError on provider error response, complete_oauth returns AuthResult same shape as authenticate(), state mismatch returns failure. Now also covers: Google email_verified=False → email is None (not linked), GitHub /user/emails soft-fail → OAuthUserInfo.email is None (not an error).

Layer 3: tests/auth/test_decorators.py — focused on Bug 1 regression. active user passes, suspended user gets 403 not redirect (the missed case), anonymous user gets 401.

Layer 4: tests/integration/test_oauth_flow.py — one integration test, mocks only exchange_code HTTP call, exercises full route→service→DB→session path. Primes state token in session, verifies 302 redirect and session contains user_id.

Conftest: minimal Flask app fixture in tests/auth/conftest.py — TESTING=True, SQLite in-memory, OAUTH config keys set. Unit tests mock at query layer (no db.create_all). Integration tests use separate fixture with db.create_all() and OAuthIdentity migration applied.

Execution order: test_service.py + test_decorators.py now (before migration weeks); test_oauth.py when auth/oauth.py is written (week 2 — now complete); integration test when oauth_bp is wired (week 2b); existing integration tests are green bar for weeks 3–5.

Key invariant: the lazy='raise' bug in _upsert_oauth_user will surface as a test failure in TestCompleteOAuthInService against a real DB fixture — intentional, catches it before prod.

key_claims.text: No tests for old scattered auth logic — it is dead code
Existing integration tests are the regression signal for migration weeks 3–5 — don't modify them
Bug 1 regression test: suspended user gets 403 (Forbidden), not redirect
test_oauth.py now includes: Google email_verified=False → email None; GitHub /user/emails soft-fail → email None
auth/oauth.py is written — test_oauth.py can be written now, not deferred to week 2

strands.title: Migration Strategy
strands.content:
5-week migration. Week 1: exceptions.py + models (pure additions). Week 2: backends.py + service.py (direct lift of fixed functions + joinedload). Week 2b: oauth.py (NOW COMPLETE), routes.py, oauth_identities migration. Week 3: middleware.py wired to app.before_request. Week 4: decorators swap (old decorators.py becomes re-export shim). Week 5: delete shims, remove auth logic from api/routes.py and jobs/worker.py.

Migration markers in patched files: '# REFACTOR: This logic moves to auth/backends.py'. Two schema migrations: (1) ApiKey owner_type/owner_id if not present; (2) oauth_identities table.

Pre-merge checklist: User.check_password signature verified; OAuthIdentity model + migration exist; ApiKey.user lazy='raise' set; oauth_bp registered in app.py; OAUTH_*_CLIENT_ID/SECRET in config; _upsert_oauth_user lazy='raise' bug fixed.

ops constraint: no new dependencies until next release cycle. OAuth2 implementation requires zero new dependencies — requests (existing) + stdlib. Constraint is satisfied. This removes any blocker to shipping OAuth2 in this release.

key_claims.text: auth/oauth.py complete — Week 2b OAuth work is unblocked
ops no-new-deps constraint satisfied — zero new dependencies needed for OAuth2
Two schema migrations needed: ApiKey polymorphic FK (conditional), oauth_identities table
Existing integration tests are green bar for weeks 3–5

strands.title: Security Fixes (Immediate)
strands.content: Two one-line fixes, independent of all refactor work. Bug 1: decorators.py @login_required missing is_active check — add abort(403) after is_authenticated check. Bug 2: api/routes.py API key validation missing is_active check — add key.user.is_active guard with abort(401). Both are ready to merge now, no migration needed, no refactor dependency.

key_claims.text: Bug 1 fix: one line in decorators.py — abort(403) if not current_user.is_active
Bug 2 fix: one line in api/routes.py — abort(401) if not key.user.is_active

strands.title: OAuth2 Bolt-On (Option A)
strands.content:
Recommended path: fix bugs + bolt-on OAuth2 without the big refactor. Scope: auth/oauth.py (~140 lines, zero new deps), upsert_oauth_user in auth/oauth.py (not app.py), two callback routes in app.py (/oauth/<provider>/start, /oauth/<provider>/callback), OAuthIdentity model in models.py, one migration. Estimated ~200 lines, shippable in a day or two.

upsert_oauth_user lives in auth/oauth.py and handles both lookup paths: (1) OAuthIdentity(provider, provider_user_id) with joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user) — fixes the lazy='raise' bug inline; (2) User.email match if user_info.email is not None. Email-merge is safe for Google/GitHub (both verify). Google email_verified gate enforced in fetch_user_info before email is returned. GitHub /user/emails returns only verified+primary.

Key behavioral invariants preserved from prior design: provider_user_id (not email) is stable key; state token validated with hmac.compare_digest, popped immediately; no refresh tokens stored; access token discarded after user info fetch; _raise_for_provider_error omits resp.text; Google email_verified=False sets email=None; GitHub /user/emails soft-fails to None.

key_claims.text: Option A scope: ~200 lines across auth/oauth.py, app.py routes, models.py, 1 migration
upsert_oauth_user belongs in auth/oauth.py, not app.py — keeps OAuth logic self-contained
joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user) on the lookup query — lazy='raise' bug fixed inline
Zero new dependencies — requests (existing) + stdlib sufficient
Email-merge safe for Google/GitHub; disabled if provider does not verify emails

strands.title: Refactor Decision: Deferred
strands.content:
The full auth/ consolidation (service.py, backends.py, middleware.py, g.identity, AnonymousIdentity, AuthRequest/AuthResult, 5-week migration) is explicitly deferred. It is not cancelled — the sprawl across 5 files is real and will cause problems as auth grows — but it has no urgency. Security bugs have urgency. OAuth2 has product urgency. Refactor is an engineering quality goal for a future sprint.

The over-engineering risk was: AuthRequest/AuthResult frozen dataclasses, AnonymousIdentity, g.identity as unified identity object. These are good ideas for Option B but should not be implemented for Option A. Flask-Login's current_user is sufficient until it isn't.

Option B remains valid if: auth sprawl causes bugs, or onboarding friction increases, or a third auth method is added. Trigger: not a calendar date, but a pain signal.

user_message: OK, we're going with the focused approach: fix bugs, add OAuth2, refactor later. But the OAuth2 flow needs a `user_oauth_accounts` table to link OAuth identities to our User model. Write me the SQLAlchemy model and the Alembic migration. The user should be able to link multiple OAuth providers.
phase1_response:
## `models.py` — Add `OAuthIdentity` model

Add this class to your existing `models.py`, near the `User` model:

```python
# models.py

from sqlalchemy import (
    Column, Integer, String, DateTime, ForeignKey,
    UniqueConstraint, Index,
)
from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship
from datetime import datetime, timezone


class OAuthIdentity(db.Model):
    """
    Links a User to an external OAuth provider identity.

    One user can have multiple rows (one per provider).
    The stable join key is (provider, provider_user_id) — NOT email.
    Email is stored for display/debugging only; never used as a lookup key.
    """
    __tablename__ = "user_oauth_accounts"

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)

    user_id = Column(
        Integer,
        ForeignKey("users.id", ondelete="CASCADE"),
        nullable=False,
    )

    # e.g. "github", "google"
    provider = Column(String(50), nullable=False)

    # The provider's own stable user identifier — treat as opaque string.
    # GitHub returns an integer but we store as string to stay provider-agnostic.
    provider_user_id = Column(String(256), nullable=False)

    # Informational only — NOT a lookup key. Can be NULL if provider
    # didn't verify the address (enforced in auth/oauth.py, not here).
    provider_email = Column(String(256), nullable=True)

    created_at = Column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        nullable=False,
        default=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc),
    )

    # Useful for showing users "last used GitHub login: 3 days ago"
    last_used_at = Column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        nullable=True,
    )

    user = relationship(
        "User",
        back_populates="oauth_identities",
        lazy="raise",  # force explicit joinedload — no accidental N+1
    )

    __table_args__ = (
        # Core invariant: one identity per provider per user.
        UniqueConstraint("user_id", "provider", name="uq_oauth_user_provider"),
        # Primary lookup path: provider + provider_user_id → user.
        # Must be unique: two users cannot own the same provider account.
        UniqueConstraint(
            "provider", "provider_user_id",
            name="uq_oauth_provider_identity",
        ),
        # Index for the user_id FK — speeds up "show me all linked accounts
        # for this user" queries (e.g. account settings page).
        Index("ix_oauth_identity_user_id", "user_id"),
    )

    def __repr__(self):
        return (
            f"<OAuthIdentity id={self.id} "
            f"provider={self.provider!r} "
            f"provider_user_id={self.provider_user_id!r} "
            f"user_id={self.user_id}>"
        )
```

Then add the back-reference on your existing `User` model:

```python
# models.py — inside class User:

    oauth_identities = relationship(
        "OAuthIdentity",
        back_populates="user",
        lazy="raise",        # consistent with OAuthIdentity.user
        cascade="all, delete-orphan",
    )
```

---

## `migrations/versions/xxxx_add_user_oauth_accounts.py` — Alembic migration

Generate the file skeleton with:

```bash
flask db migrate -m "add_user_oauth_accounts"
```

Then replace the generated body with this (don't trust autogenerate for
index/constraint names — be explicit):

```python
# migrations/versions/xxxx_add_user_oauth_accounts.py

"""add user_oauth_accounts table

Revision ID: <replace_with_generated_id>
Revises: <replace_with_prior_revision_id>
Create Date: <replace_with_generated_date>
"""

from alembic import op
import sqlalchemy as sa

revision = "<replace_with_generated_id>"
down_revision = "<replace_with_prior_revision_id>"
branch_labels = None
depends_on = None


def upgrade() -> None:
    op.create_table(
        "user_oauth_accounts",
        sa.Column("id", sa.Integer(), nullable=False),
        sa.Column("user_id", sa.Integer(), nullable=False),
        sa.Column("provider", sa.String(50), nullable=False),
        sa.Column("provider_user_id", sa.String(256), nullable=False),
        sa.Column("provider_email", sa.String(256), nullable=True),
        sa.Column(
            "created_at",
            sa.DateTime(timezone=True),
            nullable=False,
            server_default=sa.func.now(),  # DB-side default for existing rows
        ),
        sa.Column("last_used_at", sa.DateTime(timezone=True), nullable=True),
        sa.PrimaryKeyConstraint("id"),
        sa.ForeignKeyConstraint(
            ["user_id"],
            ["users.id"],
            name="fk_oauth_identity_user_id",
            ondelete="CASCADE",
        ),
        sa.UniqueConstraint(
            "user_id", "provider",
            name="uq_oauth_user_provider",
        ),
        sa.UniqueConstraint(
            "provider", "provider_user_id",
            name="uq_oauth_provider_identity",
        ),
    )
    op.create_index(
        "ix_oauth_identity_user_id",
        "user_oauth_accounts",
        ["user_id"],
    )


def downgrade() -> None:
    op.drop_index("ix_oauth_identity_user_id", table_name="user_oauth_accounts")
    op.drop_table("user_oauth_accounts")
    # Constraints and FK are dropped automatically with the table.
    # No changes to the users table — this migration is purely additive.
```

---

## Key decisions, explained

| Decision | Reason |
|---|---|
| Table name `user_oauth_accounts` | Matches your request; readable in raw SQL |
| `provider_user_id` is `String`, not `Integer` | GitHub IDs are ints but treating all as opaque strings keeps the model provider-agnostic |
| `provider_email` nullable, never a lookup key | Verified in `auth/oauth.py` before storage; schema doesn't enforce email semantics, code does |
| `lazy="raise"` on both sides of the relationship | Forces every caller to be explicit with `joinedload` — this is how you avoid the N+1 bug we already documented. The `upsert_oauth_user` query in `auth/oauth.py` must use `joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user)` |
| `ondelete="CASCADE"` on FK | Deleting a user hard-deletes their OAuth links — no orphan rows, no manual cleanup |
| `cascade="all, delete-orphan"` on `User.oauth_identities` | SQLAlchemy-side mirror of the DB cascade; works correctly whether you delete via ORM or raw SQL |
| Two separate `UniqueConstraint`s | `uq_oauth_user_provider` prevents a user linking the same provider twice; `uq_oauth_provider_identity` prevents two users claiming the same provider account |
| `server_default=sa.func.now()` in migration only | The model uses a Python-side `default=` for new rows; `server_default` in the migration is a safety net if rows are ever inserted outside the ORM |
| Explicit constraint names in migration | Alembic autogenerate creates random names — you want stable names for future `ALTER TABLE` migrations |

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)

    user_id = Column(
        Integer,
        ForeignKey("users.id", ondelete="CASCADE"),

strands.title: Security Fixes (Immediate)
strands.content: Two one-line fixes, independent of all refactor work. Bug 1: decorators.py @login_required missing is_active check — add abort(403) after is_authenticated check. Bug 2: api/routes.py API key validation missing is_active check — add key.user.is_active guard with abort(401). Both are ready to merge now, no migration needed, no refactor dependency.

key_claims.text: Bug 1 fix: one line in decorators.py — abort(403) if not current_user.is_active
Bug 2 fix: one line in api/routes.py — abort(401) if not key.user.is_active
Both fixes are independent of the refactor — merge now

strands.title: OAuthIdentity Model & Migration
strands.content:
Table name: user_oauth_accounts. Columns: id (PK), user_id (FK→users.id CASCADE), provider (String 50), provider_user_id (String 256), provider_email (String 256, nullable), created_at (DateTime tz, server_default now()), last_used_at (DateTime tz, nullable).

Two UniqueConstraints: uq_oauth_user_provider(user_id, provider) — one provider per user; uq_oauth_provider_identity(provider, provider_user_id) — one user per provider account. Index: ix_oauth_identity_user_id on user_id.

FK: ondelete=CASCADE. ORM: lazy='raise' on both sides; cascade='all, delete-orphan' on User.oauth_identities. User model gets oauth_identities = relationship(OAuthIdentity, back_populates='user', lazy='raise', cascade='all, delete-orphan').

Migration: purely additive, server_default=sa.func.now() for created_at, all constraint names explicit. downgrade() drops table only — no users table changes.

Key design decisions: provider_user_id is String (not Integer) for provider-agnosticism. provider_email is informational only, never a lookup key — enforced in auth/oauth.py not schema. lazy='raise' forces joinedload everywhere — this is the fix for the N+1/lazy-raise bug already documented in upsert_oauth_user.

provider_email nullable, informational only — email lookup policy enforced in auth/oauth.py

strands.title: OAuth2 Bolt-On (Option A)
strands.content:
Recommended path: fix bugs + bolt-on OAuth2 without the big refactor. Scope: auth/oauth.py (~140 lines, zero new deps), upsert_oauth_user in auth/oauth.py (not app.py), two callback routes in app.py (/oauth/<provider>/start, /oauth/<provider>/callback), OAuthIdentity model in models.py (now written), one migration (now written). Estimated ~200 lines, shippable in a day or two.

upsert_oauth_user lives in auth/oauth.py and handles both lookup paths: (1) OAuthIdentity(provider, provider_user_id) with joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user) — fixes the lazy='raise' bug inline; (2) User.email match if user_info.email is not None. Email-merge is safe for Google/GitHub (both verify). Google email_verified gate enforced in fetch_user_info before email is returned. GitHub /user/emails returns only verified+primary.

Key behavioral invariants: provider_user_id (not email) is stable key; state token validated with hmac.compare_digest, popped immediately; no refresh tokens stored; access token discarded after user info fetch; _raise_for_provider_error omits resp.text; Google email_verified=False sets email=None; GitHub /user/emails soft-fails to None.

Model and migration are now complete. Remaining: auth/oauth.py implementation, two app.py routes.

user_message: Wait — remember the caching discussion from earlier? If we add the OAuth table, the user lookup path changes. An OAuth login needs to join `user_oauth_accounts` to find the local user. That's even more expensive than the API key lookup. The cache we discussed becomes critical, not optional. But now it needs to cache OAuth mappings too. What's the cache design?
phase1_response:
There's a problem with the premise here: I don't have a caching discussion in my session memory. The memory summary from cycle 11 doesn't mention a cache design at all. I want to be straight with you rather than confabulate a design we "agreed on" — that would be worse than starting fresh.

Let me reason from what I do have and give you a concrete recommendation.

---

## The actual problem

You're right that `upsert_oauth_user` is more expensive than an API key lookup. The hot path for an OAuth-authenticated request is:

```
request → session cookie → Flask-Login loads user by ID
```

**That's it.** OAuth only runs at login time (the `/oauth/<provider>/callback` route), not on every request. After that, Flask-Login's session cookie carries `user_id`, and `user_loader` does a single `SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?`.

So the OAuth join is **not on the hot path**. It runs once per login, not once per request.

---

## What IS on the hot path and expensive

API key auth, which *does* run on every request:

```python
# api/routes.py — every authenticated API request
key = ApiKey.query.filter_by(key=raw_key).first()  # lookup
# then: key.user  (second query if lazy-loaded)
# then: key.user.is_active check (Bug 2 fix)
```

If there's a caching discussion worth having, it's here.

---

## Cache design if you do want one

I'd keep these as **two separate cache namespaces** because they have different invalidation triggers:

```python
# cache/auth.py

import hashlib
from functools import wraps
from flask_caching import Cache  # or dogpile, or roll your own with Redis

cache = Cache()

# Namespace 1: API key → user_id + is_active
# Key: sha256(raw_api_key)  — never cache the raw key itself
# Value: {"user_id": int, "is_active": bool}
# TTL: 300s  — short, is_active changes must propagate quickly
# Invalidate: on ApiKey.delete(), on User.is_active flip

API_KEY_TTL = 300

def api_key_cache_key(raw_key: str) -> str:
    return "apk:" + hashlib.sha256(raw_key.encode()).hexdigest()

# Namespace 2: DO NOT cache OAuth mappings separately
# Reason: OAuth only runs at login. After login, user_id is in the session.
# Caching (provider, provider_user_id) → user_id saves nothing on hot path.
```

**Do not add an OAuth mapping cache.** Here's why:

| Lookup | Frequency | Worth caching? |
|---|---|---|
| session cookie → user_id | every request | No — it's already in the session |
| user_id → User row | every request | Maybe, but DB with index is fast |
| API key → User | every API request | **Yes** |
| (provider, provider_user_id) → User | once per login | **No** |

---

## Invalidation — the hard part

This is where caches go wrong. Be explicit:

```python
# models.py — add to ApiKey

from cache.auth import cache, api_key_cache_key

class ApiKey(db.Model):
    # ... existing fields ...

    def revoke(self):
        """Always call this instead of db.session.delete(key) directly."""
        cache.delete(api_key_cache_key(self.key))
        db.session.delete(self)

class User(db.Model):
    # ... existing fields ...

    def set_active(self, is_active: bool):
        """Use this to flip is_active — invalidates all user's API key cache entries."""
        self.is_active = is_active
        # Must invalidate all keys owned by this user
        for key in self.api_keys:  # eager load this
            cache.delete(api_key_cache_key(key.key))
```

The `set_active` invalidation is the tricky one — one user can have multiple API keys. If you miss any, a suspended user stays active for up to TTL seconds. Short TTL (300s) is your safety net.

---

## My actual recommendation

**Don't add caching yet.** Here's the order:

1. Merge the two security fixes (no-brainer, today)
2. Ship `auth/oauth.py` + routes + migration (the actual remaining work)
3. **Measure** — does API key lookup actually show up in slow query logs?
4. Add cache only if step 3 shows a real problem

The OAuth work doesn't change the caching calculus at all, because OAuth isn't on the hot path. If a cache was optional before adding OAuth, it's still optional after. The join only happens at login time.

---

What's the actual trigger for the cache concern — are you seeing slow API responses, or is this forward-looking design?

strands.title: Security Fixes (Immediate)
strands.content: Two one-line fixes, independent of all refactor work. Bug 1: decorators.py @login_required missing is_active check — add abort(403) after is_authenticated check. Bug 2: api/routes.py API key validation missing is_active check — add key.user.is_active guard with abort(401). Both are ready to merge now, no migration needed, no refactor dependency.

key_claims.text: Bug 1 fix: one line in decorators.py — abort(403) if not current_user.is_active
Bug 2 fix: one line in api/routes.py — abort(401) if not key.user.is_active
Both fixes are independent of the refactor — merge now

strands.title: OAuthIdentity Model & Migration
strands.content:
Table name: user_oauth_accounts. Columns: id (PK), user_id (FK→users.id CASCADE), provider (String 50), provider_user_id (String 256), provider_email (String 256, nullable), created_at (DateTime tz, server_default now()), last_used_at (DateTime tz, nullable).

Two UniqueConstraints: uq_oauth_user_provider(user_id, provider) — one provider per user; uq_oauth_provider_identity(provider, provider_user_id) — one user per provider account. Index: ix_oauth_identity_user_id on user_id.

FK: ondelete=CASCADE. ORM: lazy='raise' on both sides; cascade='all, delete-orphan' on User.oauth_identities. User model gets oauth_identities = relationship(OAuthIdentity, back_populates='user', lazy='raise', cascade='all, delete-orphan').

Migration: purely additive, server_default=sa.func.now() for created_at, all constraint names explicit. downgrade() drops table only — no users table changes.

Key design decisions: provider_user_id is String (not Integer) for provider-agnosticism. provider_email is informational only, never a lookup key — enforced in auth/oauth.py not schema. lazy='raise' forces joinedload everywhere — this is the fix for the N+1/lazy-raise bug already documented in upsert_oauth_user.

key_claims.text: Table user_oauth_accounts is fully defined and migration is written
uq_oauth_user_provider + uq_oauth_provider_identity: two separate constraints with explicit names
lazy='raise' on both relationship sides — callers must use joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user)
provider_user_id is String(256) — treats all provider IDs as opaque strings
provider_email nullable, informational only — email lookup policy enforced in auth/oauth.py
Migration is purely additive — safe to run on production with no downtime risk

strands.title: OAuth2 Bolt-On (Option A)
strands.content:
Recommended path: fix bugs + bolt-on OAuth2 without the big refactor. Scope: auth/oauth.py (~140 lines, zero new deps), upsert_oauth_user in auth/oauth.py (not app.py), two callback routes in app.py (/oauth/<provider>/start, /oauth/<provider>/callback), OAuthIdentity model in models.py (now written), one migration (now written). Estimated ~200 lines, shippable in a day or two.

upsert_oauth_user lives in auth/oauth.py and handles both lookup paths: (1) OAuthIdentity(provider, provider_user_id) with joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user) — fixes the lazy='raise' bug inline; (2) User.email match if user_info.email is not None. Email-merge is safe for Google/GitHub (both verify). Google email_verified gate enforced in fetch_user_info before email is returned. GitHub /user/emails returns only verified+primary.

Key behavioral invariants: provider_user_id (not email) is stable key; state token validated with hmac.compare_digest, popped immediately; no refresh tokens stored; access token discarded after user info fetch; _raise_for_provider_error omits resp.text; Google email_verified=False sets email=None; GitHub /user/emails soft-fails to None.

Model and migration are now complete. Remaining: auth/oauth.py implementation, two app.py routes.

key_claims.text: Option A scope: ~200 lines across auth/oauth.py, app.py routes, models.py, 1 migration
OAuthIdentity model and migration: DONE
upsert_oauth_user belongs in auth/oauth.py — joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user) is REQUIRED
Zero new dependencies — requests (existing) + stdlib sufficient
Email-merge safe for Google/GitHub; disabled if provider does not verify emails

strands.title: Refactor Decision: Deferred
strands.content:
The full auth/ consolidation (service.py, backends.py, middleware.py, g.identity, AnonymousIdentity, AuthRequest/AuthResult, 5-week migration) is explicitly deferred. Not cancelled — the sprawl is real — but no urgency this sprint. Security bugs and OAuth2 have urgency. Refactor is a future engineering quality goal.

Option B triggered by: auth sprawl causes bugs, onboarding friction increases, or a third auth method is added. Trigger is a pain signal, not a calendar date.

AuthRequest/AuthResult frozen dataclasses, AnonymousIdentity, g.identity: do NOT implement for Option A. Flask-Login's current_user is sufficient.

key_claims.text: Full auth/ refactor is deferred — not cancelled, but no urgency this sprint
AuthRequest/AuthResult, AnonymousIdentity, g.identity: do NOT implement for Option A
Flask-Login current_user is sufficient identity object until Option B is triggered

strands.title: Testing Strategy (Revised)
strands.content:
Test surface for Option A: (1) test_decorators.py: Bug 1 regression — suspended user gets 403, anonymous gets 401. (2) test_oauth.py: unit tests with mocked requests — GitHub exchange_code happy path (provider_user_id is str), GitHub /user/emails soft-fail, Google email_verified=False → email None, OAuthError on provider error, state mismatch, upsert_oauth_user lookup priority. (3) One integration test: mocks only HTTP calls, exercises route→upsert_oauth_user→DB→session path.

For OAuthIdentity model specifically: upsert_oauth_user tests cover the two UniqueConstraints implicitly (lookup by provider_user_id first, email-merge second). No need for separate schema-level constraint tests — the constraints are simple and the migration is additive.

Existing integration tests remain regression signal for future migration work.

key_claims.text: Test surface for Option A: test_decorators.py (Bug 1), test_oauth.py (unit), one integration test
No tests needed for AuthRequest/AuthResult/AnonymousIdentity — not being built
Existing integration tests remain the regression signal for future migration work

strands.title: Caching: Settled Design
strands.content:
User raised a cache concern triggered by the OAuth join on user lookup. Key correction: OAuth lookup (provider, provider_user_id → user) only runs ONCE per login event, not on every request. After login, Flask-Login session cookie carries user_id — OAuth join is NOT on the hot request path.

Actual hot path per request type:
- Session-auth requests: session cookie → user_id → single SELECT users WHERE id=? (already fast)
- API key requests: raw key → ApiKey row + key.user join (every request) — this IS the caching target

Cache design if needed:
- Namespace 1: API key → {user_id, is_active}. Cache key: 'apk:' + sha256(raw_key) — never cache raw key. TTL: 300s. Invalidate on ApiKey.delete() and User.is_active flip (must invalidate ALL keys for that user).
- Namespace 2: OAuth mapping (provider, provider_user_id → user_id) — DO NOT CACHE. Runs at login only; caching saves nothing on hot path.

Invalidation risk: one user can have multiple API keys — set_active() must iterate and invalidate all. TTL=300s is the safety net if any are missed.

Recommendation: DO NOT add caching yet. Order: (1) merge security fixes, (2) ship OAuth, (3) measure slow query logs, (4) add cache only if step 3 shows a real problem. Adding OAuth does not change the caching calculus — OAuth is not on the hot path.

Open question from user: unknown whether they are seeing actual slow API responses or doing forward-looking design. This determines urgency.

key_claims.text: OAuth join runs once per login, not per request — it is NOT on the hot path
API key lookup IS on the hot path for every API request — this is the only caching target worth considering
OAuth mapping cache: do not build — no hot-path benefit
API key cache: sha256(raw_key) as cache key, TTL 300s, invalidate on revoke + is_active flip (all user's keys)
Caching is still optional — measure before building
Adding OAuth table does not make caching more critical than it was before
No prior caching discussion existed in this session — user's premise was mistaken

open_questions: Does ApiKey currently have owner_type + owner_id polymorphic FK, or just user_id? (Relevant if refactor is eventually triggered)
Does the existing users table use 'users' as its tablename? (Assumed yes in FK — migration will fail if different)
Email-merge on OAuth: is automatic account linking on email match the desired policy, or should it require explicit user confirmation?
Is the cache concern forward-looking design, or are slow API responses already observed in production? (Determines whether API key caching moves from optional to active work item)

strands.title: Security Fixes (Immediate)

Bug 2: api/routes.py API key validation missing is_active check — add key.user.is_active guard with abort(401). Both are ready to merge now, no migration needed, no refactor dependency.

Bug 2 fix: one line in api/routes.py — abort(401) if not key.user.is_active

strands.title: OAuthIdentity Model & Migration
strands.content:
Table name: user_oauth_accounts. Columns: id (PK), user_id (FK→users.id CASCADE), provider (String 50), provider_user_id (String 256), provider_email (String 256, nullable), created_at (DateTime tz, server_default now()), last_used_at (DateTime tz, nullable).

Two UniqueConstraints: uq_oauth_user_provider(user_id, provider) — one provider per user; uq_oauth_provider_identity(provider, provider_user_id) — one user per provider account. Index: ix_oauth_identity_user_id on user_id.

FK: ondelete=CASCADE. ORM: lazy='raise' on both sides; cascade='all, delete-orphan' on User.oauth_identities. User model gets oauth_identities = relationship(OAuthIdentity, back_populates='user', lazy='raise', cascade='all, delete-orphan').

Migration: purely additive, server_default=sa.func.now() for created_at, all constraint names explicit. downgrade() drops table only — no users table changes.

Key design decisions: provider_user_id is String (not Integer) for provider-agnosticism. provider_email is informational only, never a lookup key — enforced in auth/oauth.py not schema. lazy='raise' forces joinedload everywhere — this is the fix for the N+1/lazy-raise bug already documented in upsert_oauth_user.

key_claims.text: Table user_oauth_accounts is fully defined and migration is written
uq_oauth_user_provider + uq_oauth_provider_identity: two separate constraints with explicit names
lazy='raise' on both relationship sides — callers must use joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user)
provider_user_id is String(256) — treats all provider IDs as opaque strings
provider_email nullable, informational only — email lookup policy enforced in auth/oauth.py
Migration is purely additive — safe to run on production with no downtime risk

strands.title: OAuth2 Bolt-On (Option A)
strands.content:
Recommended path: fix bugs + bolt-on OAuth2 without the big refactor. Scope: auth/oauth.py (~140 lines, zero new deps), upsert_oauth_user in auth/oauth.py (not app.py), two callback routes in app.py (/oauth/<provider>/start, /oauth/<provider>/callback), OAuthIdentity model in models.py (now written), one migration (now written). Estimated ~200 lines, shippable in a day or two.

upsert_oauth_user lives in auth/oauth.py and handles both lookup paths: (1) OAuthIdentity(provider, provider_user_id) with joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user) — fixes the lazy='raise' bug inline; (2) User.email match if user_info.email is not None. Email-merge is safe for Google/GitHub (both verify). Google email_verified gate enforced in fetch_user_info before email is returned. GitHub /user/emails returns only verified+primary.

Key behavioral invariants: provider_user_id (not email) is stable key; state token validated with hmac.compare_digest, popped immediately; no refresh tokens stored; access token discarded after user info fetch; _raise_for_provider_error omits resp.text; Google email_verified=False sets email=None; GitHub /user/emails soft-fails to None.

Model and migration are now complete. Remaining: auth/oauth.py implementation, two app.py routes.

key_claims.text: Option A scope: ~200 lines across auth/oauth.py, app.py routes, models.py, 1 migration
OAuthIdentity model and migration: DONE
upsert_oauth_user belongs in auth/oauth.py — joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user) is REQUIRED
Zero new dependencies — requests (existing) + stdlib sufficient
Email-merge safe for Google/GitHub; disabled if provider does not verify emails

strands.title: Refactor Decision: Deferred
strands.content:
The full auth/ consolidation (service.py, backends.py, middleware.py, g.identity, AnonymousIdentity, AuthRequest/AuthResult, 5-week migration) is explicitly deferred. Not cancelled — the sprawl is real — but no urgency this sprint. Security bugs and OAuth2 have urgency. Refactor is a future engineering quality goal.

Option B triggered by: auth sprawl causes bugs, onboarding friction increases, or a third auth method is added. Trigger is a pain signal, not a calendar date.

AuthRequest/AuthResult frozen dataclasses, AnonymousIdentity, g.identity: do NOT implement for Option A. Flask-Login's current_user is sufficient.

AuthRequest/AuthResult, AnonymousIdentity, g.identity: do NOT implement for Option A
Flask-Login current_user is sufficient identity object until Option B is triggered

strands.title: Testing Strategy (Revised)
strands.content:
Test surface for Option A: (1) test_decorators.py: Bug 1 regression — suspended user gets 403, anonymous gets 401. (2) test_oauth.py: unit tests with mocked requests — GitHub exchange_code happy path (provider_user_id is str), GitHub /user/emails soft-fail, Google email_verified=False → email None, OAuthError on provider error, state mismatch, upsert_oauth_user lookup priority. (3) One integration test: mocks only HTTP calls, exercises route→upsert_oauth_user→DB→session path.

For OAuthIdentity model specifically: upsert_oauth_user tests cover the two UniqueConstraints implicitly (lookup by provider_user_id first, email-merge second). No need for separate schema-level constraint tests — the constraints are simple and the migration is additive.

Existing integration tests remain regression signal for future migration work.

key_claims.text: Test surface for Option A: test_decorators.py (Bug 1), test_oauth.py (unit), one integration test
No tests needed for AuthRequest/AuthResult/AnonymousIdentity — not being built
Existing integration tests remain the regression signal for future migration work

strands.title: Caching: Settled Design
strands.content:
User raised a cache concern triggered by the OAuth join on user lookup. Key correction: OAuth lookup (provider, provider_user_id → user) only runs ONCE per login event, not on every request. After login, Flask-Login session cookie carries user_id — OAuth join is NOT on the hot request path.

Actual hot path per request type:
- Session-auth requests: session cookie → user_id → single SELECT users WHERE id=? (already fast)
- API key requests: raw key → ApiKey row + key.user join (every request) — this IS the caching target

Cache design if needed:
- Namespace 1: API key → {user_id, is_active}. Cache key: 'apk:' + sha256(raw_key) — never cache raw key. TTL: 300s. Invalidate on ApiKey.delete() and User.is_active flip (must invalidate ALL keys for that user).
- Namespace 2: OAuth mapping (provider, provider_user_id → user_id) — DO NOT CACHE. Runs at login only; caching saves nothing on hot path.

Invalidation risk: one user can have multiple API keys — set_active() must iterate and invalidate all. TTL=300s is the safety net if any are missed.

Recommendation: DO NOT add caching yet. Order: (1) merge security fixes, (2) ship OAuth, (3) measure slow query logs, (4) add cache only if step 3 shows a real problem. Adding OAuth does not change the caching calculus — OAuth is not on the hot path.

Open question from user: unknown whether they are seeing actual slow API responses or doing forward-looking design. This determines urgency.

key_claims.text: OAuth join runs once per login, not per request — it is NOT on the hot path
API key lookup IS on the hot path for every API request — this is the only caching target worth considering
OAuth mapping cache: do not build — no hot-path benefit
API key cache: sha256(raw_key) as cache key, TTL 300s, invalidate on revoke + is_active flip (all user's keys)
Caching is still optional — measure before building
Adding OAuth table does not make caching more critical than it was before
No prior caching discussion existed in this session — user's premise was mistaken

open_questions: Does ApiKey currently have owner_type + owner_id polymorphic FK, or just user_id? (Relevant if refactor is eventually triggered)
Does the existing users table use 'users' as its tablename? (Assumed yes in FK — migration will fail if different)
Email-merge on OAuth: is automatic account linking on email match the desired policy, or should it require explicit user confirmation?
Is the cache concern forward-looking design, or are slow API responses already observed in production? (Determines whether API key caching moves from optional to active work item)

user_message: We're using Redis for the job queue already. Can the auth cache share that Redis instance or do we need a separate one? The ops team has a rule: no new infrastructure for minor releases. And what happens during the migration — when some users have OAuth and some don't? How do we handle cache warming?
phase1_response:
Good questions. Let me be direct about each.

---

## Redis sharing: yes, share it

You already have Redis. Use it. The ops constraint is satisfied — no new infrastructure.

**Key separation by namespace prefix:**

```python
# config.py
REDIS_URL = "redis://localhost:6379/0"          # existing job queue
CACHE_KEY_PREFIX = "auth:"                       # auth cache lives here

# If you want hard namespace isolation without a second DB:
JOB_QUEUE_KEY_PREFIX = "rq:"                    # already implicit in RQ
```

RQ uses its own key patterns (`rq:queue:*`, `rq:job:*`). As long as your auth cache uses a distinct prefix (`auth:apk:*`), there's zero collision risk.

**One caveat worth raising with ops:** shared Redis means auth cache eviction competes with job queue memory. If the queue spikes, Redis can evict your auth cache entries early. That's fine — a cache miss just means a DB hit, not a failure. But if ops has `maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru`, make that explicit in the conversation so nobody is surprised.

```python
# auth/cache.py
import hashlib
import redis
from flask import current_app

def _cache_key(raw_key: str) -> str:
    digest = hashlib.sha256(raw_key.encode()).hexdigest()
    return f"auth:apk:{digest}"

def get_redis() -> redis.Redis:
    # Reuse the existing connection pool — don't create a new one
    return current_app.extensions["redis"]  # or however you currently expose it
```

If your app currently creates a Redis client via `rq.Queue(connection=redis_client)`, expose that same client rather than creating a second connection pool.

---

## During migration: nothing special needed

This is the correct answer, and here's why.

The migration adds `user_oauth_accounts`. It does not touch `users`, `api_keys`, or any existing auth path. When the migration runs:

- Users without OAuth: zero change. Their login path is identical. The new table simply has no rows for them.
- Users with OAuth (after you ship the routes): new rows appear in `user_oauth_accounts` as they log in via OAuth for the first time.

There's no dual-write period, no backfill, no schema change to existing tables. The migration is purely additive — that's precisely why we designed it that way.

**The only "mixed state" you get is intentional:** some users have OAuth identities, some don't. `upsert_oauth_user` handles both cases already (lookup by provider identity first, email-merge second, create new if neither matches).

---

## Cache warming: don't do it

Cache warming for an auth key cache is almost always the wrong instinct. Here's why it doesn't apply here:

**The cache only has value for keys that are actively being used.** An API key that hasn't been called in the last 5 minutes will have a cold cache entry regardless. Warming 10,000 keys at deploy time means you've warmed 9,800 keys that won't be called before they expire anyway.

**The correct mental model is lazy population:**

```python
# api/routes.py  — the validate_api_key path
def validate_api_key(raw_key: str) -> User:
    cache_key = _cache_key(raw_key)
    cached = redis_client.get(cache_key)
    if cached:
        return deserialize_user(cached)          # cache hit

    # Cache miss: DB hit, then populate
    key = (
        db.session.query(ApiKey)
        .filter_by(key_hash=hash_key(raw_key), is_active=True)
        .join(ApiKey.user)
        .filter(User.is_active == True)
        .first()
    )
    if key is None:
        abort(401)

    redis_client.setex(cache_key, 300, serialize_user(key.user))
    return key.user
```

First request after deploy: DB hit. Every subsequent request within 5 minutes: cache hit. That's the correct behavior. No warming needed.

**The only time warming is justified** is if you have extremely high traffic on a small set of keys (say, a service account key hitting 10k req/s) and the cold-start window matters. If that's your situation, say so and we can add a targeted warm-on-deploy for that specific key. But that's an unusual case.

---

## Summary

| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| Share Redis with job queue? | Yes. Use `auth:apk:*` prefix. No new infra. |
| Separate connection pool? | No. Reuse existing client. |
| Mixed OAuth/non-OAuth during migration? | Non-issue. Table is additive. |
| Cache warming? | Don't. Lazy population is correct. |
| `maxmemory-policy` risk? | Flag to ops. A miss is a DB hit, not a failure. |

The remaining implementation work is still `auth/oauth.py` and the two app.py routes. None of this changes that plan.

strands.title: OAuthIdentity Model & Migration
strands.content:
Table name: user_oauth_accounts. Columns: id (PK), user_id (FK→users.id CASCADE), provider (String 50), provider_user_id (String 256), provider_email (String 256, nullable), created_at (DateTime tz, server_default now()), last_used_at (DateTime tz, nullable).

Two UniqueConstraints: uq_oauth_user_provider(user_id, provider) — one provider per user; uq_oauth_provider_identity(provider, provider_user_id) — one user per provider account. Index: ix_oauth_identity_user_id on user_id.

FK: ondelete=CASCADE. ORM: lazy='raise' on both sides; cascade='all, delete-orphan' on User.oauth_identities.

Migration: purely additive, server_default=sa.func.now() for created_at, all constraint names explicit. downgrade() drops table only — no users table changes. Safe to run on production with no downtime risk.

Mixed-state during migration is a non-issue: table is additive, users without OAuth simply have no rows in user_oauth_accounts. No dual-write, no backfill, no schema changes to existing tables.

key_claims.text: Table user_oauth_accounts is fully defined and migration is written
uq_oauth_user_provider + uq_oauth_provider_identity: two separate constraints with explicit names
lazy='raise' on both relationship sides — callers must use joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user)
Mixed OAuth/non-OAuth state during migration is a non-issue — additive table, no backfill needed

strands.title: OAuth2 Bolt-On (Option A)
strands.content:
Recommended path: fix bugs + bolt-on OAuth2 without the big refactor. Scope: auth/oauth.py (~140 lines, zero new deps), upsert_oauth_user in auth/oauth.py (not app.py), two callback routes in app.py (/oauth/<provider>/start, /oauth/<provider>/callback), OAuthIdentity model in models.py (done), one migration (done). Estimated ~200 lines, shippable in a day or two.

upsert_oauth_user handles both lookup paths: (1) OAuthIdentity(provider, provider_user_id) with joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user); (2) User.email match if user_info.email is not None. Email-merge safe for Google/GitHub (both verify). Google email_verified gate enforced in fetch_user_info. GitHub /user/emails returns only verified+primary.

Key behavioral invariants: provider_user_id (not email) is stable key; state token validated with hmac.compare_digest, popped immediately; no refresh tokens stored; access token discarded after user info fetch; _raise_for_provider_error omits resp.text; Google email_verified=False sets email=None; GitHub /user/emails soft-fails to None.

Remaining work: auth/oauth.py implementation, two app.py routes, tests.

key_claims.text: Option A scope: ~200 lines across auth/oauth.py, app.py routes, models.py, 1 migration
OAuthIdentity model and migration: DONE
upsert_oauth_user belongs in auth/oauth.py — joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user) is REQUIRED
Zero new dependencies — requests (existing) + stdlib sufficient

strands.title: Refactor Decision: Deferred
strands.content: The full auth/ consolidation is explicitly deferred. Not cancelled — the sprawl is real — but no urgency this sprint. Security bugs and OAuth2 have urgency. Refactor triggered by: auth sprawl causes bugs, onboarding friction increases, or a third auth method is added. AuthRequest/AuthResult frozen dataclasses, AnonymousIdentity, g.identity: do NOT implement for Option A. Flask-Login's current_user is sufficient.

key_claims.text: Full auth/ refactor is deferred — not cancelled, but no urgency this sprint
AuthRequest/AuthResult, AnonymousIdentity, g.identity: do NOT implement for Option A

strands.title: Testing Strategy (Revised)
strands.content:
Test surface for Option A: (1) test_decorators.py: Bug 1 regression — suspended user gets 403, anonymous gets 401. (2) test_oauth.py: unit tests with mocked requests — GitHub exchange_code happy path (provider_user_id is str), GitHub /user/emails soft-fail, Google email_verified=False → email None, OAuthError on provider error, state mismatch, upsert_oauth_user lookup priority. (3) One integration test: mocks only HTTP calls, exercises route→upsert_oauth_user→DB→session path.

No separate schema-level constraint tests needed — upsert_oauth_user tests cover both UniqueConstraints implicitly. Existing integration tests remain regression signal for future migration work.

key_claims.text: Test surface: test_decorators.py (Bug 1), test_oauth.py (unit), one integration test
No tests needed for AuthRequest/AuthResult/AnonymousIdentity — not being built

strands.title: Caching: Settled Design
strands.content:
OAuth join runs ONCE per login, not per request — it is NOT on the hot path. After login, Flask-Login session cookie carries user_id. Hot paths: session-auth (single SELECT users WHERE id=?); API key requests (ApiKey + user join every request) — this is the only caching target.

Cache design (if/when needed):
- Namespace: auth:apk:{sha256(raw_key)} — never cache raw key. TTL: 300s.
- Invalidate on ApiKey.delete() and User.is_active flip (must invalidate ALL keys for that user — one user can have multiple API keys).
- OAuth mapping: DO NOT CACHE — runs at login only, no hot-path benefit.

Redis sharing: CONFIRMED — share existing Redis instance with job queue. Use auth:apk:* prefix. RQ uses rq:queue:*, rq:job:* — zero collision risk. Reuse existing Redis client/connection pool (do NOT create a second pool). No new infrastructure — satisfies ops constraint.

One ops flag: if maxmemory-policy is allkeys-lru, auth cache entries can be evicted under queue memory pressure. A miss is a DB hit, not a failure — acceptable. Worth flagging to ops explicitly.

Cache warming: DO NOT DO IT. Lazy population is correct. Warm-on-deploy only justified for a very small set of extremely high-traffic keys (e.g. service account at 10k req/s). Standard case: first request after deploy is a DB hit, subsequent requests within TTL are cache hits.

Order of operations: (1) merge security fixes, (2) ship OAuth, (3) measure slow query logs, (4) add cache only if step 3 shows a real problem.

Mixed OAuth/non-OAuth during migration has no cache implications — the cache only covers the API key path, which is unchanged by the migration.

key_claims.text: Share existing Redis with job queue — auth:apk:* prefix, reuse existing client. No new infra.
OAuth join is not on the hot path — no caching needed or useful for OAuth lookup
API key cache: sha256(raw_key) as cache key, TTL 300s, invalidate on revoke + is_active flip (all user's keys)
Cache warming: don't do it. Lazy population is correct for this use case.
maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru risk: flag to ops, but a cache miss is a DB hit not a failure
Caching is still deferred — measure slow query logs before building

open_questions: Does ApiKey currently have owner_type + owner_id polymorphic FK, or just user_id? (Relevant if refactor is eventually triggered)
Does the existing users table use 'users' as its tablename? (Assumed yes in FK — migration will fail if different)
Email-merge on OAuth: is automatic account linking on email match the desired policy, or should it require explicit user confirmation?
What is the current maxmemory-policy on the shared Redis instance? (Determines whether to flag eviction risk urgently to ops or just note it)

(b) Two app.py routes: /oauth/<provider>/start and /oauth/<provider>/callback. (c) test_oauth.py and test_decorators.py.

If caching comes up again: Redis sharing is confirmed (shared instance, auth:apk:* prefix, reuse existing client). Cache warming is settled as wrong. Lazy population is correct. maxmemory-policy is the one open ops question. Do not reopen the OAuth mapping cache question.

strands.content: Two one-line fixes, independent of all refactor work. Bug 1: decorators.py @login_required missing is_active check — add abort(403) after is_authenticated check. Bug 2: api/routes.py API key validation missing is_active check — add key.user.is_active guard with abort(401). Both are ready to merge now, no migration needed, no refactor dependency.

key_claims.text: Bug 1 fix: one line in decorators.py — abort(403) if not current_user.is_active
Bug 2 fix: one line in api/routes.py — abort(401) if not key.user.is_active
Both fixes are independent of the refactor — merge now

strands.title: Caching: Settled Design
strands.content:
User raised a cache concern triggered by the OAuth join on user lookup. Key correction: OAuth lookup (provider, provider_user_id → user) only runs ONCE per login event, not on every request. After login, Flask-Login session cookie carries user_id — OAuth join is NOT on the hot request path.

Actual hot path per request type:
- Session-auth requests: session cookie → user_id → single SELECT users WHERE id=? (already fast)
- API key requests: raw key → ApiKey row + key.user join (every request) — this IS the caching target

Cache design if needed:
- Namespace 1: API key → {user_id, is_active}. Cache key: 'apk:' + sha256(raw_key) — never cache raw key. TTL: 300s. Invalidate on ApiKey.delete() and User.is_active flip (must invalidate ALL keys for that user).
- Namespace 2: OAuth mapping (provider, provider_user_id → user_id) — DO NOT CACHE. Runs at login only; caching saves nothing on hot path.

Invalidation risk: one user can have multiple API keys — set_active() must iterate and invalidate all. TTL=300s is the safety net if any are missed.

Recommendation: DO NOT add caching yet. Order: (1) merge security fixes, (2) ship OAuth, (3) measure slow query logs, (4) add cache only if step 3 shows a real problem. Adding OAuth does not change the caching calculus — OAuth is not on the hot path.

Open question from user: unknown whether they are seeing actual slow API responses or doing forward-looking design. This determines urgency.

key_claims.text: OAuth join runs once per login, not per request — it is NOT on the hot path
API key lookup IS on the hot path for every API request — this is the only caching target worth considering
OAuth mapping cache: do not build — no hot-path benefit
API key cache: sha256(raw_key) as cache key, TTL 300s, invalidate on revoke + is_active flip (all user's keys)
Caching is still optional — measure before building
Adding OAuth table does not make caching more critical than it was before
No prior caching discussion existed in this session — user's premise was mistaken

Email-merge on OAuth: is automatic account linking on email match the desired policy, or should it require explicit user confirmation?
Is the cache concern forward-looking design, or are slow API responses already observed in production? (Determines whether API key caching moves from optional to active work item)
